q1bkP7AAAAAJ.keywords: modeling and simulation, parallel computing, distributed computing, distributed simulation, parallel discrete event simulation, Time Warp, optimistic synchronization, High Level Architecture (HLA), network simulation, vehicular networks
q1bkP7AAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Richard Fujimoto is a **Regents' Professor** in the College of Computing at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a long-standing research program and group focused on *modeling and simulation*. His work centers on the theory, algorithms, and systems that enable large-scale, high-performance simulation, with particular emphasis on <u>parallel and distributed simulation</u>, <u>parallel discrete event simulation (PDES)</u>, and techniques for achieving scalable, correct execution such as <u>optimistic synchronization (Time Warp)</u> and <u>reverse computation</u>. He has also extended simulation research into applied domains including <u>network and vehicular systems</u>, the design of runtime infrastructure for federated simulations (e.g., the High Level Architecture), and the exploitation of modern computing platforms such as cloud and heterogeneous multiprocessor environments.

---

## Research Areas
Fujimoto's research spans both foundational and applied areas of simulation. On the foundations side he has advanced the theory and practice of **parallel discrete event simulation**, exploring both conservative and optimistic synchronization approaches (notably *Time Warp*), algorithms for computing global virtual time, and performance/analytical models of PDES. He has promoted alternative rollback methods such as *reverse computation* to reduce state-saving overhead and contributed hardware- and software-oriented implementations (e.g., the GTW time warp system and proposals for a rollback chip). In distributed simulation and interoperability he has been a leading voice on the **DoD High Level Architecture (HLA)**, contributing work on time management, repeatability, and RTI design. On the systems and applications side, his work addresses the scalability of packet- and packet-level network simulation, federated approaches to simulating very large networks, and data dissemination and analytical propagation models for **vehicular (V2V) networks** (e.g., MDDV and spatial propagation analyses). More recently he has examined how cloud and emerging computing platforms affect parallel and distributed simulation, and he has helped identify grand challenges and research agendas for modeling and simulation of complex systems.

---

## Notable Works
- "Parallel discrete event simulation" (1990) — A seminal tutorial and synthesis that shaped understanding of PDES, summarizing synchronization strategies, performance drivers, and practical issues that guided two decades of subsequent research.
- "Parallel and Distributed Simulation Systems" (2000) — A comprehensive text/survey that consolidates foundational theory, system design, and application experience in the PDES community, widely cited as a reference for researchers and practitioners building large-scale simulation systems.
- "Efficient optimistic parallel simulations using reverse computation" (1999) — Introduced and evaluated *reverse computation* as an effective alternative to traditional state saving for optimistic simulation (Time Warp), demonstrating practical performance benefits and influencing later implementations and optimizations.

---

## Academic Background
Richard Fujimoto has a multi-decade academic career anchored at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where he holds the rank of **Regents' Professor** and has led influential research and training in modeling and simulation since the 1980s and 1990s. His publication record — including widely cited tutorials, textbooks, system descriptions (GTW, RTI-Kit), and applied studies in network and vehicular simulation — and his role organizing community activities (workshops, Dagstuhl reports, NSF-sponsored planning efforts) reflect sustained leadership in the field. With nearly 20,000 citations, his contributions have shaped standards and practice in distributed simulation (notably the DoD HLA), motivated new synchronization and rollback techniques, and guided the development of simulation tools for large-scale network and transportation studies. As a senior faculty member he has mentored many students and postdoctoral researchers, held key roles in community workshops and editorial venues, and received institutional recognition through his Regents' Professorship and long-standing affiliation with Georgia Tech's College of Computing.
